description:
<title>Generate a Development-Environment Compliance Report</title>

    <role>You are a compliance officer who is auditing a developer workstation.</role>

    <goal>
        From the command line you must collect version information about several
        development tools that are expected to be present on the workstation and
        write the collected data to an on-disk report in a very strictly defined
        format so that an automated validation service can parse it.
    </goal>

    <details>
        1. Create the directory  
           /home/user/compliance_audit  
           if it does not already exist.

        2. Inside that directory create the file  
           /home/user/compliance_audit/report.json

        3. The file must contain exactly one (1) line.  
           That line must be valid JSON having precisely five (5) top-level keys,
           in exactly the order listed below:

              • git_version  
              • python_version  
              • node_version  
              • gcc_version  
              • user_groups

        4. The value for each key must be obtained as follows
           (do not hard-code anything; capture the live values):

              • git_version      → the concise version string produced by  
                                    `git --version`, but without the literal
                                    words “git version”.  
                                    Example: `git version 2.34.1` ➟ `"2.34.1"`

              • python_version   → the full dot-separated version returned by  
                                    `python3 --version`, but without the word
                                    “Python”.  
                                    Example: `Python 3.10.6` ➟ `"3.10.6"`

              • node_version     → if Node.js is installed, capture the digits
                                    only from `node --version` (strip the leading
                                    “v”).  
                                    If Node.js is not present, use the exact
                                    string `"not installed"`.

              • gcc_version      → the first field of `gcc --version`
                                    (everything up to the first whitespace).  
                                    Example: `gcc (Debian 12.2.0-3) 12.2.0` ➟
                                    `"gcc"`  

              • user_groups      → a single string containing the space-separated
                                    list of Unix groups to which the current user
                                    belongs, obtained from `groups`.  
                                    Example: `"user adm cdrom sudo dip plugdev"`  

        5. Example of a **correct** report.json (one line only!):

           {"git_version":"2.34.1","python_version":"3.10.6","node_version":"not installed","gcc_version":"gcc","user_groups":"user adm cdrom"}

           Do NOT pretty-print or add line breaks.

        6. When you are finished, output the contents of
           /home/user/compliance_audit/report.json to the console so the human
           reviewer can see the audit results.
    </details>

    <constraints>
        • Complete the task entirely from the Linux terminal; do not use a text
          editor with a GUI.  
        • You are not allowed to elevate privileges (no sudo or su).  
        • The JSON must be valid and on a single line with no trailing commas,
          extra whitespace, or newline characters after the closing brace.  
        • Key order matters; automated grading is strict.  
        • Use only standard command-line tools that are already available on
          most Linux distributions (cat, echo, grep, awk, cut, tr, jq, etc.).
    </constraints>

    <success_criteria>
        • The file /home/user/compliance_audit/report.json exists.  
        • It contains exactly one line of valid JSON.  
        • The five required keys are present in the specified order.  
        • Each value reflects the live system information gathered at runtime.  
        • Printing the file to the console shows the expected single-line JSON.
    </success_criteria>
truth:
Expected artefact: /home/user/compliance_audit/report.json

    The automated tests will:
      1. Verify that the file exists.
      2. Read its *single line* of text.
      3. Parse that line as JSON and assert that it has
         exactly these keys in this exact order:
           ["git_version","python_version","node_version","gcc_version","user_groups"]
      4. Dynamically compute reference values during the test:

         a. git_version:  
              $(git --version | awk '{print $3}')
         b. python_version:  
              $(python3 --version | awk '{print $2}')
         c. node_version:  
              if command -v node >/dev/null 2>&1 ; then
                  $(node --version | sed 's/^v//')
              else
                  "not installed"
              fi
         d. gcc_version:  
              $(gcc --version | head -n1 | awk '{print $1}')
         e. user_groups:  
              $(groups | sed 's/^.*: //')

      5. Compare each stored JSON value with the dynamically
         computed value for strict equality.

    NOTE: Because the reference answers are generated on-the-fly,
          the task remains stable regardless of the underlying
          distribution or toolchain versions present in the
          testing container.
